text: robotic right hemicolectomy with intracorporeal anastomosis by Dr. Andrew Uraniha.

robotic right hemicolectomy with intracorporeal anastomosis by Dr. Andrew Uraniha. This is a sixty-seven-year-old gentleman who previously underwent a robotic appendectomy. However, the pathology came back as adenocarcinoma of the appendix. Therefore, he required to have a completion right hemicolectomy for staging and removing the lymph nodes.

This is a sixty-seven-year-old gentleman who previously underwent a robotic appendectomy. However, the pathology came back as adenocarcinoma of the appendix. Therefore, he required to have a completion right hemicolectomy for staging and removing the lymph nodes.
The surgeon isolates the ileocolic vessels, freeing them from surrounding tissue in preparation for further dissection.

The surgeon isolates the ileocolic vessels and performs a VVLT lateral approach to mobilize the mesentery and right colon from the retroperitoneum towards the right upper quadrant. The right ureter and duodenum are identified and protected during this dissection.

The surgeon uses a VVLT lateral approach to free the mesentery and right colon from the retroperitoneum, progressing towards the right upper quadrant. The right ureter and duodenum are identified and protected during this dissection.
The surgeon identifies the heliocollis vessels, isolates them completely, and then clips and divides them.

The surgeon identifies and isolates the heliocollis vessels, which are then clipped and divided. The lateral peritoneal attachments of the terminal ileum and right colon are mobilized. Scar tissue from a previous appendectomy is excised and included in the specimen.
The surgeon mobilizes the lateral peritoneal attachments of the terminal ileum and right colon, excising scar tissue from a previous appendectomy and incorporating it into the specimen.
The surgeon staples off the thermal field using a 3.5mm staple, followed by complete stapling of the white column from the transfer scroller with multiple firings of the 3.5mm staple.
The surgeon staples off the thermal field using a 3.5 mm staple. The white column is then completely stapled off from the transfer scroller with multiple firings of the 3.5 mm staple.
The surgical specimen is placed above the liver and then into a retrieval bag. It is removed from the abdomen through one of the trocar sites and sent to pathology for permanent diagnosis.

The specimen is placed above the liver and later placed into a bag for removal from the abdomen through one of the trochoid sites. The terminal ileum is positioned next to the transverse colon to perform a primary intracorporeal anastomosis.
The surgeon positions the terminal ileum adjacent to the transverse colon to perform a primary intracorporeal anastomosis, preparing the bowel segments for connection.
The surgeon creates an anastomosis between the transverse colon and terminal ileum using two sequential firings of a 3.5mm stapler, ensuring the connection between these sections of the bowel.
The surgeon creates an anastomosis between the transverse colon and terminal ileum using two sequential firings of a 3.5 millimeter stapler.

The surgeon closes the opening of the anastomosis using a 2-0 absorbable V-lock suture in a running fashion. The suture line is then reinforced with 2-0 Ethibond sutures in a simple, interrupted pattern as a second layer.

The surgeon closes the opening of the anastomosis using a 2-0 absorbable V-lock suture in a running fashion. The suture line is reinforced with additional simple interrupted stitches using Ethibond as a second layer.
The surgeon closes the mesenteric defect using a two-oh V-lock suture in a simple running fashion.
The surgeon closes the mesenteric defect using a two-oh V-lock suture in a simple running fashion.
